# `cfgs`

## Simple, correct handling of config, data and cache files

Like everyone else, I wrote a lot of programs which saved config files
as dotfiles in the user's home directory like `~/.my-program-name` and now
everyone's home directory has dozens of these.

Then I read
[this article](https://0x46.net/thoughts/2019/02/01/dotfile-madness/).

Great was my embarrasment to discover that there was a
[neat little specification](https://specifications.freedesktop.org/basedir-spec/basedir-spec-latest.html)
for data, config and cache directories in Linux that prevents this problem, and
that I was not using it:

So I implemented a small and simple Python API as a single file, `cfgs.py`.

It works on all versions of Python from 2.7 to 3.7, has complete test coverage,
and all the functionality is reachable from a single class, `cfgs.App`

## How it works in one sentence

Create a `cfgs.App` for your application, project, or script which
handles finding, reading and writing your data and config files, and
managing your cache directories.

## How to install

You can either use pip:

```bash
pip install cfgs
```

Or if you don't like dependencies (and who does?), you can drop the source file
[cgfs.py](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/timedata-org/cfgs/master/cfgs.py)
right into your project.

## Usage examples

```python
import cfgs
app = cfgs.App('my-project')
print(app.xdg.XDG_CACHE_HOME)
#   /home/tom/.cache/my-project

app.xdg.XDG_CONFIG_DIRS
#   /etc/xdg

with app.config.open() as f:
    f.update(name='oliver', species='dog')
    f['description'] = {'size': 'S', 'fur': 'brown'}
    print(f.filename)
#    /home/tom/.cache/my-project/my-project.json

# Later:
with app.config.open() as f:
    print(f['name'])
#    oliver

    print(f.as_dict())
#     {'name': 'oliver', 'species': 'dog',
#      'description': {'size': 'S', 'fur': 'brown'}
```

## Cache

```python
import cfgs
cache_size = 0x10000000
app = cfgs.App('my-project')
directory = app.cache.directory(cache_size=cache_size)
# TODO: rewrite cache or add features.
```

## Using `cfgs` In legacy code

If you already have code to handle your config, data and cache files, then you
can just use `cgfs` to get the
[XDG variables](https://specifications.freedesktop.org/basedir-spec/basedir-spec-latest.html)

```python
from cfgs import XDG

xdg = XDG()
config_dir = xdg.XDG_CONFIG_HOME

# Your code here - eg:
my_config_file = os.path.join(config_dir, 'my-file.json')
with open(my_config_file) as f:
    legacy_write_my_file(f)
```

`cfgs` automatically handles data and config files, and independently, cache
directories.

## API Documentation

API documentation is [here](https://timedata-org.github.io/cfgs/cfgs.html).

modules.name: cfgs
documentation:
`cfgs`: ⚙ Serializable hierarchical dataclass settings ⚙

Also:

Simple, correct handling of config, data and cache files.
Fully compliant with the XDG Base Directory Specification.

variables.name: cfgs.SUFFIX_TO_FORMAT
documentation:
Map file suffixes to the file format - the partial inverse
to `cfgs.FORMAT_TO_SUFFIX`

variables.name: cfgs.FORMAT_TO_SUFFIX
documentation:
Map file formats to file suffix - the partial inverse
to `cfgs.SUFFIX_TO_FORMAT`

variables.name: cfgs.FORMATS
documentation: A list of all formats that `cfgs` understands.

classes.name: cfgs.App

methods.name: cfgs.App.__init__
documentation:
Arguments:

  name: the name of the App.  `name` is used as a directory
        name so it should not contain any characters illegal in
        pathnames

  format: the format for config and data files from this App

class_variables.name: cfgs.App.DEFAULT_FORMAT
documentation: The default, default file format for all Apps

`cfg.App` is the main class, but it has no methods - it just holds the
`config`, `data`, `cache` and `xdg` objects.

classes.name: cfgs.XDG

methods.name: cfgs.XDG.__init__
documentation: Construct the class with a snapshot of the six XDG base directories

The XDG Base Directory Spec mandates six directories for config and data
files, caches and runtime files, with default values that can be overridden
through environment variables.  This class takes a snapshot of these six
directories using the current environment.

classes.name: cfgs.Directory

methods.name: cfgs.Directory.__init__
documentation:
Don't call this constructor directly - use either
`cfgs.App.config` or `cfgs.App.data` instead.

methods.name: cfgs.Directory.open
documentation:
Open a persistent `cfg.File`.

Arguments:
  filename: The name of the persistent file. If None,
    `filename` defaults to `cfg.App.name` plus the format suffix

  format: A string representing the file format.  If None,
     first try to guess the filename from the filename, then use
     `self.format`

methods.name: cfgs.Directory.all_files
documentation:
Yield all filenames matching the argument in either the home
directory or any of the search directories

methods.name: cfgs.Directory.full_name
documentation: Return the full name of a file with respect to this XDG directory
An XDG directory of persistent, formatted files

classes.name: cfgs.File

methods.name: cfgs.File.__init__
documentation:
Do not call this constructor directly but use
`cfg.Directory.open` instead

methods.name: cfgs.File.read
documentation: Re-read the contents from the file

methods.name: cfgs.File.write
documentation: Write the contents to the file

methods.name: cfgs.File.as_dict
documentation: Return a deep copy of the contents as a dict

methods.name: cfgs.File.clear
documentation: Clear the contents without writing

A formatted data or config file where you can set and get items,
and read or write.

classes.name: cfgs.Cache

methods.name: cfgs.Cache.__init__
documentation: Do not call this constructor - instead use `cfgs.App.cache`

methods.name: cfgs.Cache.directory
documentation:
Return a `cfgs.CacheDirectory`

Arguments:
  name: The relative pathname of the cache directory

  cache_size: The number of bytes allowed in the cache.
      The default of 0 means "unlimited cache size"
A class that creates caches

methods.name: cfgs.CacheDirectory.__init__
documentation: Do not call this constructor - use `cfgs.Cache.directory`

methods.name: cfgs.CacheDirectory.open
documentation:
Open a cached file in this directory.

If the file already exists, it is opened for read.

Otherwise the cache is pruned and the file is opened for write.

Arguments:
  filename: the name of the file, relative to the cache directory
  size_guess: A guess as to how large the file will be, in bytes
  binary: if True, the file is opened in binary mode

methods.name: cfgs.CacheDirectory.prune
documentation:
Prune the cache to generate at least `bytes_needed` of free space,
if this is possible.

methods.name: cfgs.Format.read
documentation: Read contents from an open file in this format

methods.name: cfgs.Format.write
documentation: Write contents in this format to an open file

methods.name: cfgs.Format.create
documentation: Return new, empty contents

methods.name: cfgs.Format.as_dict
documentation: Convert the contents to a dict

methods.name: cfgs.ConfigparserFormat.read
documentation: Read contents from an open file in this format

methods.name: cfgs.ConfigparserFormat.write
documentation: Write contents in this format to an open file

methods.name: cfgs.ConfigparserFormat.create
documentation: Return new, empty contents

methods.name: cfgs.ConfigparserFormat.as_dict
documentation: Convert the contents to a dict

documentation: The name of the configparser format
